channel.title: Philadelphia Experiment on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in Philadelphia Experiment on Alien Truth Social

item.title: Morris K. Jessup
item.description:
Morris K. Jessup (1900–1959) was an American astronomer and ufologist who gained notoriety for his 1955 book, The Case for the UFO. In his writing, Jessup proposed that unidentified flying objects were extraterrestrial in origin and linked them to ancient archaeological sites and prehistoric civilizations. He later became a central figure in the legend of the “Philadelphia Experiment” after receiving correspondence from Carl Meredith Allen, which detailed claims of a secret U.S. Navy naval invisibility project. Jessup died by suicide in Florida on April 20, 1959, following a period of personal instability and declining professional success.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morris_K._Jessup

item.title: William L. Moore and His Contributions to UFOlogy
item.description:
William L. Moore, born in 1943, is an author and former UFO researcher known for his work from the late 1970s to the late 1980s. He co-authored books with Charles Berlitz, including “The Philadelphia Experiment - Project Invisibility” and “The Roswell Incident,” which alleged extraterrestrial involvement in the Roswell incident. Moore also circulated the Majestic 12 documents, which purported to reveal a secret government group overseeing UFOs. Critics have described his books as unsubstantiated and his work on the Majestic 12 documents as fraudulent.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Moore_%28ufologist%29

item.title: Morris Ketchum Jessup
item.description:
Morris Ketchum Jessup was an American ufologist with a Master of Science degree in astronomy. He is best known for his writings on UFOs, including his 1955 book The Case for the UFO. Jessup’s work explored theories linking ancient monuments to prehistoric superscience and speculated about the nature of UFOs. His death in 1959 was ruled a suicide, but circumstances surrounding it have fueled conspiracy theories, particularly concerning the Philadelphia Experiment.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morris_K._Jessup
